text: This is a video presentation of endocyanin green fluorescence guided robotic-assisted transthoracic esophagectomy to evaluate the role of ICG-INR fluorescence imaging in real-time identification of thoracic duct during surgery and also to assess gastric cardiovascularity.
The dose of ICG used is two point five milligram per mm.
For thoracic duct identification site of injection is either first web space of bilateral lower limb or bilateral superficial inguinal lymph node.
For gastric cardiovascularity intravenous injection is used.
Under ultrasound guidance, the surgeon identifies the bilateral superficial inguinal lymph nodes and injects 1 ml of indocyanine green (ICG) into each node.
The patient is positioned prone, and the surgeon begins the infra-esophageal dissection anteriorly, allowing the esophagus to hang from the roof of the surgical field.
The surgeon divides the inferior pulmonary ligament and continues dissection up to the diaphragm's crossing and the lower gastroesophageal junction.
The surgeon carefully dissects the subcarinal lymph node, taking precautions to avoid thermal injury to the adjacent right and left main bronchus.
The surgeon performs posterior dissection and identifies the thoracic duct using ICG Firefly technology, which highlights the duct for visual confirmation.
The surgeon completes the infra-esophageal dissection under fluorescence guidance, with the esophagus visibly suspended from the roof of the surgical field.
The surgeon begins dissection in the supra-azygous region, continuing the surgical procedure with careful manipulation of the surrounding tissues.
The surgeon dissects the right paratracheal lymph nodes, ensuring careful preservation of the right recurrent laryngeal nerve while clearing station six lymph nodes.
The surgeon begins dissection on the left side, identifying and carefully dissecting around the left recurrent laryngeal nerve.
The surgeon uses ICG fluorescence to identify the thoracic duct, which lies in close proximity to the left recurrent laryngeal nerve, ensuring preservation and avoiding injury during dissection.
The surgeon dissects lymph nodes located over the subclavian vessels, carefully separating them from surrounding structures.
The surgeon performs a slow and careful dissection in the supra-azygous region, taking precautions to avoid thermal injury to the membranous trachea.
The surgeon clips and divides the azygos vein, completing the separation of this vessel.
The surgeon confirms the intact thoracic duct and completes the mobilization of the intrathoracic esophagus, ensuring the esophagus is free from surrounding structures.
The patient is positioned supine, and the surgeon begins the intra-abdominal dissection.
The surgeon incises the lesser omentum and continues dissection up to the diaphragm, revealing the anatomical structures in this region.
The surgeon divides the gastrosplenic ligament containing the short gastric vessels using a vessel sealer, visibly separating the ligament from surrounding structures.
The surgeon opens the periodic capsule and dissects the lymph nodes at stations eight and twelve, carefully separating them from surrounding tissue.
The surgeon seals and divides the left gastric vessels at their origin using a vessel sealer, ensuring clear separation from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon completes the DII dissection, achieving clearance of station XI P and XI D lymph nodes, with visible removal of lymphatic tissue.
The surgeon divides the greater omentum while carefully preserving the gastroepiploic arcade, ensuring the vascular integrity of the arcade is maintained during the dissection.
The surgeon creates the gastric conduit using a linear stapler, forming the conduit from the stomach tissue.
Three milliliters of dye are injected intravenously, and the enhanced vascularity of the gastric tissue is visually confirmed.
The surgeon performs a side quadrangle esophagogastric anastomosis in the neck using a powered stapler, connecting the esophagus and gastric conduit.
In our series of one sixty eight patients, anastomotic leak rates were two point nine percent and chylic rates were one point eight percent.
Use of ICG fluorescence is easy and safe method for real time identification and preservation of thoracic duct.
It also helps in assessing gastric conduit viability to avoid anastomotic leak.
Thank you for watching this video.
